title: Historical Thinking
objective: Students will explore historical thinking concepts including Evidence from the Past, Vocabulary: historical thinking, Different Accounts of the Same Event.

steps.content: Let's explore Evidence from the Past! Have you ever wondered about this?
Understand that everything we know about the past comes from evidence — objects, buildings, pictures, documents, and stories that have survived
Let me show you something cool. Wineburg historical thinking framework
When you learns about something that happened long ago, do they ask how we know about it — and understand that someone had to find or leave behind that evidence?
Great job! Today we learned about Evidence from the Past. Wineburg historical thinking framework Historical Thinking in the Elementary Years (ERIC)
Today we're going to learn about Vocabulary: historical thinking. Get ready for an adventure!

Know and use the vocabulary of historical thinking — source, evidence, primary source, secondary source, artefact, chronology, chronological order, BC/BCE, AD/CE, century, decade, era, period, timeline, excavation, archaeologist, interpretation, corroborate, bias, perspective — and apply these terms when discussing how we know about the past and how reliable our knowledge is
Time to try this out: Correctly use BC/BCE and AD/CE to place events in time and explain what the letters stand for
If you asked you whether something they read in a history book was definitely true, could they explain why it might not be — using words like 'source', 'evidence', or 'bias'?

Great job! Today we learned about Vocabulary: historical thinking. Correctly use BC/BCE and AD/CE to place events in time and explain what the letters stand for Distinguish between a primary source and a secondary source with an example of each

lessons.title: Different Accounts of the Same Event

steps.content: Have you ever thought about Different Accounts of the Same Event? Let's find out together!
Recognise that different people can give different accounts of the same event — and that both can be genuine while still disagreeing
Let's practice together! Wineburg corroboration heuristic

If you heard two different versions of the same historical event, would they understand that both people might be telling the truth from their own point of view — and want to know why the versions differ?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Different Accounts of the Same Event. Wineburg corroboration heuristic Initiating Historical Thinking in Elementary Schools
